coredata.dc:title: A rapid and simple method based on manganese oxide nanosheets/tetramethyl benzidine system oxidation activity for bisulfite screening in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bayat-Afshary, Farbod
Tavoussi-Shirazi, Delaram-Sadat
Bagheri, Habib
coredata.dc:description: Bisulfite is a food additive with preservative and antioxidant properties; however, excessive use can severely damage nutritional compounds in food, reducing nutritional value. Moreover, it can lead to the development of sensitivities, asthma, localized heart anemia, and lung cancer. Therefore, it is essential to establish a simple, innovative, fast, and reliable method for bisulfite identification in foodstuffs. In this research, a novel fluorescence method based on manganese oxide nanosheets and carbon dots is designed for bisulfite detection in food samples. Initially, manganese oxide nanosheets were synthesized via a bottom-up approach, and carbon dots are produced using a hydrothermal synthesis method. The manganese oxide nanosheets oxidize tetramethyl benzidine, quenching the fluorescence emission of carbon dots. In the presence of bisulfite, oxidized tetramethyl benzidine is reduced, leading to the recovery of carbon dot fluorescence emission. Thus, bisulfite could be identified through the fluorescence signal of carbon dots. The linear range of the method varies from 0.2 to 2.5 mg L−1, with a detection limit of 0.06 mg L−1. Additionally, the developed method exhibits a rapid response time (< 30 s), good sensitivity, and selectivity. The method is applied to measure bisulfite in food samples such as water, vinegar, sugar, and macaroni, showing relative recovery between 90 % and 106 %, and a relative standard deviation of less than 5 %. These results demonstrate the successful performance of this method compared to previous similar approaches.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Manganese oxide nanosheets
Enzyme mimic activity
Fluorescence spectroscopy
Carbon dots
Bisulfite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of MnO2 NSs and CDs
Principal and mechanism of bisulfite detection
Detection of bisulfite in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors greatly appreciate the support from Sharif University of Technology (Grant no. QA990417).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of bisulfite determination protocol.

(a) TEM images of MnO2 NSs, (b) EDX spectrum, elemental mapping (manganese: green, oxygen: pink, carbon: red), weight and atomic percentages of elements in MnO2 NSs, (c) EDX spectrum, weight and atomic percentages of elements in CDs, (d) FT-IR spectrum of MnO2 NSs, (e) FT-IR spectrum of synthesized CDs, (f) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs.

(a) The UV-Vis absorption, fluorescence emission, and excitation spectra of CDs. Inset: From left to right, the solution of CDs under visible light and the solution of CDs under UV light at 365 nm, (b) The UV-Vis spectrum of the diamine/diimine complex (blue) and the diimine form (yellow), (c) The overlaid absorption spectrum of OxTMB with the emission spectrum of CDs, (d) The fluorescence spectrum of CDs in the presence of OxTMB and bisulfite, excited at 350 nm. Inset: From left to right, solutions of CDs, CDs with OxTMB, and CDs with OxTMB in the presence of bisulfite under visible light.

The selectivity of the method over possible interfering species. AA, GSH, NO2 -, Fe2+ and Fe3+, 10 mg L−1; HSO3 -, 1 mg L−1; others, 100 mg L−1; CD is a reference for maximum signal. Error bar represents the standard deviation for three determinations.
The analytical parameters for determination of bisulfite.
The results of the analysis of real liquid samples.
The results of the analysis of real solid samples.
Comparison of the current method with previous relevant reports.

head.ce:title: A rapid and simple method based on manganese oxide nanosheets/tetramethyl benzidine system oxidation activity for bisulfite screening in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental and Bio-Analytical Laboratories, Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, P.O. Box 11365-9516, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Environmental and Bio-Analytical Laboratories, Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental and Bio-Analytical Laboratories, Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, P.O. Box 11365-9516, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bisulfite is a food additive with preservative and antioxidant properties; however, excessive use can severely damage nutritional compounds in food, reducing nutritional value. Moreover, it can lead to the development of sensitivities, asthma, localized heart anemia, and lung cancer. Therefore, it is essential to establish a simple, innovative, fast, and reliable method for bisulfite identification in foodstuffs. In this research, a novel fluorescence method based on manganese oxide nanosheets and carbon dots is designed for bisulfite detection in food samples. Initially, manganese oxide nanosheets were synthesized via a bottom-up approach, and carbon dots are produced using a hydrothermal synthesis method. The manganese oxide nanosheets oxidize tetramethyl benzidine, quenching the fluorescence emission of carbon dots. In the presence of bisulfite, oxidized tetramethyl benzidine is reduced, leading to the recovery of carbon dot fluorescence emission. Thus, bisulfite could be identified through the fluorescence signal of carbon dots. The linear range of the method varies from 0.2 to 2.5 mg L−1, with a detection limit of 0.06 mg L−1. Additionally, the developed method exhibits a rapid response time (< 30 s), good sensitivity, and selectivity. The method is applied to measure bisulfite in food samples such as water, vinegar, sugar, and macaroni, showing relative recovery between 90 % and 106 %, and a relative standard deviation of less than 5 %. These results demonstrate the successful performance of this method compared to previous similar approaches.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data regarding this manuscript are provided.

ce:section.ce:para: Food additives, whether natural or synthetic, play a crucial role in enhancing various aspects of food products, such as flavor, color, and shelf life. In the European Union, these additives are identified by a three-digit code beginning with the letter E, indicating their functional category. Categorically, food additives are classified into 25 groups, encompassing colorants, preservatives, antioxidants, and sweeteners. Preservatives, in particular, safeguard food against microorganisms, extending its shelf life and ensuring safety throughout production, packaging, transportation, and storage processes. While most additives pose no health risk in controlled amounts, exceeding permissible limits can jeopardize human health (Wu et al., 2022).

Introduced in Japan in 1948, bisulfite (HSO3 -) has gained widespread use globally due to its preservative and antioxidant properties in food (Hayasaka et al., 2017). By preventing enzymatic reactions and bacterial growth during storage, bisulfite serves as an effective preservative for items like fruits. Additionally, its antioxidant attributes safeguard foods like dried fruits, beverages, and sugar against oxidative reactions (Taylor et al., 1986). However, excessive usage can compromise the nutritional content of food and lead to severe health issues, including sensitivities, asthma, localized heart anemia, and lung cancer (Jiang et al., 2022).

Regulation of sulfite usage in food items is mandated by the World Health Organization, with permissible limits set at 10 mg kg−1 in countries such as Australia, New Zealand, Canada, the United Kingdom, the United States, and the European Union. Surpassing this limit requires the declaration of sulfite content on food packaging. The daily consumption limit for sulfites is further established at 0.7 mg kg−1 of body weight. Following adverse events in 1986, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in the United States prohibited the use of sulfites in raw food items. Given these considerations, the development of a simple, innovative, rapid, and reliable method for the identification of sulfites in food is essential. Various methods have been devised for sulfite detection, including flow injection analysis (Hassan et al., 2006), chromatography (Xing et al., 2021), colorimetry (Song et al., 2021), electrochemistry (Liu et al., 2017a), and fluorescence (Peng et al., 2022). Notably, ion chromatography and liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry are recognized as standard methods by Association of Official Analytical Chemists (AOAC) and U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), respectively, for measuring sulfite ions in food samples.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their unique optical, physical, and chemical properties. Generally, CDs refer to very small and light-absorbing carbon nanostructures with sizes less than 10 nm, including carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and polymer dots (Zhu et al., 2015). CDs consist of carbon core structures surrounded by various functional groups such as carboxyl, amide, and hydroxyl. These functional groups impart various properties, including high solubility, easy functionalization, excellent stability in biological environments, and biocompatibility, to CDs (Li and Dong, 2018; Wang et al., 2010). Beyond these properties, the simplicity of synthesis and low cost have led to widespread applications of CDs in various fields, including chemical and biological sensors, antibacterial agents, drugs, fluorescence imaging, photocatalysts, and security (Manshaei et al., 2021; Wang and Lu, 2022; Zhu et al., 2015).

Natural enzymes are highly efficient and selective biocatalysts present in the bodies of all living organisms. However, the specific environmental conditions for their function, synthesis, and purification pose challenges for their use in biosensors. Therefore, replacing these enzymes with nanomaterials has gained significant attention. In recent years, some nanomaterials have been introduced as nanoenzymes with behaviors similar to natural enzymes such as oxidase, peroxidase, superoxidase, and catalase. The large surface area, low cost, stability, high catalytic efficiency, and diverse properties of these nanomaterials make them a suitable option for the development of artificial enzymes (Liu et al., 2017b; Singh et al., 2020; Zhong et al., 2018). Among these nanomaterials, MnO2 nanostructures are utilized in various sensors and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) systems due to their significant enzymatic activity. In recent years, MnO2 nanostructures have been reported to exhibit enzyme-like activity similar to oxidase enzymes. These nanostructures can oxidize 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) without the need for natural enzyme horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and hydrogen peroxide. Therefore, MnO2-based sensors, in conjunction with TMB, can play a crucial role in the identification of various targets (Lai et al., 2017). In recent years, numerous sensors based on CDs and MnO2 nanostructures have been developed for the detection of analytes including glutathione (GSH) (He et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2015), ascorbic acid (AA) (Hu et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2016), uric acid (UA) (Amjadi et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018), hydrazine (Hiremath et al., 2020), hydrogen peroxide (Ning et al., 2020; Su et al., 2023), and organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) (Yan et al., 2018). Generally, in the design of sensors based on MnO2 nanostructures, two approaches exist: fluorescence sensors and colorimetric sensors, both utilizing the oxidase-like properties of MnO2. In the fluorescence sensors, MnO2 nanostructures, owing to their optical properties, can quench the fluorescence emission of fluorophores, including carbon dots, through mechanisms like Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE). The presence of the analyte can directly or indirectly lead to the reduction of MnO2 to Mn2+, therefor disrupting the quenching mechanism (Ali et al., 2020; Deng et al., 2011; Yan et al., 2018).

The aim of this study is to design a novel, rapid, and simple fluorescence method based on MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) and Blue CDs for the identification of bisulfite preservatives in food samples. Accordingly, oxidized TMB was used as an alternative to MnO2 NSs for quenching CDs, and bisulfite ions reacted with the oxidized TMB instead of MnO2 NSs. This innovative approach enhances the method’s response time and sensitivity. To achieve this, MnO2 NSs were synthesized using a bottom-up approach, and CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis. MnO2 NSs oxidize TMB to form a yellow-colored compound that quenches the fluorescence emission of CDs. In the presence of bisulfite ions, the oxidized TMB is reduced, restoring the fluorescence emission of CDs and enabling the identification of bisulfite through the fluorescence signal.

The following chemicals were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany): potassium permanganate (KMnO4, 99 %), anhydrous citric acid (CA, 99 %), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99 %), sodium acetate (NaOAc, 99 %), glacial acetic acid (HOAc, 99.7 %), sodium nitrate (NaNO3, 99 %), sodium nitrite (NaNO2, 99 %), calcium chloride (CaCl2, 99 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 99 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99 %), potassium chloride (KCl, 99 %), copper(II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2.2 H2O, 95 %), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99 %), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4, 99 %), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, 99 %), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, 98 %), lithium chloride (LiCl, 97 %), lithium hypochlorite (LiClO, 99 %), barium chloride (BaCl2, 99 %), potassium perchlorate (KClO4, 99 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), sodium bisulfite (NaHSO3, 99 %). Ascorbic acid (AA), reduced glutathione (GSH for biochemistry), tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 99 %), and 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid 1-hydrate (MES, BioUltra with purity 99.5 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Real samples, including white vinegar, sugar, vermicelli, mineral water, and macaroni, were obtained from a local supermarket.

Laboratory equipment included a magnetic stirrer (HMS 1115, Ideal Pars), analytical balance (Mettler, Model 200 AE, Switzerland), pH meter (Metrohm Herisau E520, Switzerland), and an ultrasonic bath (Elma, Germany) for the synthesis and dispersion of MnO2 NSs. A centrifuge (LABOFUGE 15000) was used for the synthesis of MnO2 NSs. Fluorescence spectra and signals were recorded using a Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer, while UV-Vis spectra were recorded by a PerkinElmer Lambda 25 spectrophotometer.

In order to synthesize MnO2 NSs, the bottom-up method was employed according to previous study, utilizing the reaction of KMnO4 and MES (Shi et al., 2019). For this purpose, 5 mL of KMnO4 solution (0.1 M) were added to 50 mL of MES buffer (0.1 M, pH=6.0). After 30 min in an ultrasonic bath, MnO2 NSs were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm. The collected particles were washed five times with deionized water and then dried in a freeze dryer for 24 h. In the final step, the obtained MnO2 NSs were dispersed in Ultrapure water to prepare a stock solution with a concentration of 3600 mg L−1 and stored at 4°C.

For the synthesis of blue CDs, citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were employed according to a previous study (Schneider et al., 2017). Specifically, 10.57 g of CA and 0.30 g of EDA were dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water to form a transparent solution. In the next step, the solution was transferred to a 150 mL sealed Teflon autoclave, heated at 200°C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the synthesized CDs were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove aggregated particles. Subsequently, the supernatant was dried in a freeze dryer for 24 h. Finally, 0.85 g of the obtained CDs were dissolved in 10 mL of Ultrapure water to prepare a stock solution with a concentration of 85,000 mg L−1 and stored at 4°C.

A 100 µL TMB solution (832 µM) was added to 140 µL of MnO2 NSs solution (828 µM). This mixture was allowed to react for 1 min to ensure complete oxidation of TMB (final oxidized TMB concentration of 347 µM). Subsequently, 1 mL of different concentration of bisulfite solution was transferred to a 2 mL microtube, and 100 µL of NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.0) and 100 µL of CDs solution (85 mg L−1) were added. In the next step, 100 µL of the oxidized TMB solution were added to the mixture (final concentration of oxidized TMB and CDs are 26.7 µM and 6.5 mg L−1, respectively), and after 30 s, the fluorescence signal of CDs was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The schematic representation of the determination process is shown in Fig. 1.

All food samples were purchased at a local supermarket. For the extraction of bisulfite from solid samples, initially, 2–5 g of the sample was mixed with 10 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was then subjected to ultrasonic bath treatment for 15 min, followed by stirring for 1 h. Following this, the obtained mixture was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 10 min, and was passed through 0.22 µm filter. Finally, the resulting solution was diluted with NaAc-HAc buffer (pH=4.0). To prepare liquid samples, the sample was first passed through 0.22 µm filter, and ultimately it was diluted with NaAc-HAc buffer (pH=4.0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MnO2 NSs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the characterization of CDs and MnO2 NSs, TEM images, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), FT-IR spectra, and UV-Vis spectra were utilized.

Fig. 2a displays TEM images of the synthesized MnO2 NSs. According to these images, the synthesized nanostructures appear as two-dimensional nanosheets with lateral dimensions around 100 nm and a thickness in the range of 1.5–2 nm.

Fig. 2b illustrates the EDX spectrum and the composition of MnO2 NSs. The results of the EDX test confirm the high purity of the synthesized nanosheets and the uniform distribution of elements. The stoichiometric ratio of oxygen to manganese has also been maintained. The presence of carbon atoms in the EDX spectrum is due to the use of MES buffer for the synthesis of MnO2 NSs.
The EDX spectrum and the composition of CDs are shown in Fig. 2c. The results confirm the presence of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen in the structure of the synthesized CDs.

The FT-IR spectrum of MnO2 NSs is presented in Fig. 2d. The broad band in the region of 3400 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O-H bonds. The band at 1620 cm−1 is related to the bending vibrations of O-H bonds combined with Mn atoms. The band in the range of 430–500 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibrations of Mn-O bonds (Di et al., 2017; Qu et al., 2020).

Fig. 2e depicts the FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized CDs. The broad bands in the region of 2800–3400 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of alcohol (-OH) and amine groups (-NH2) on the surface of CDs. The bands in the range of 1650–1700 cm−1 correspond to CO stretching vibrations of carbonyl group of the amide and carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs (Schneider et al., 2017). The bands at 1383 cm−1 and 770 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of CH3 groups. The stretching vibrations of C-N and N-H are observed in the regions of 1045 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1, confirming the presence of amide and amine groups on the surface of CDs (Manshaei et al., 2021).

The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs is shown in Fig. 2f, indicating a broad absorption range from 200 to 500 nm with a peak at 340 nm due to the d-d transition of Mn(IV) ions in MnO2 NSs (He et al., 2015).

Fig. 3a illustrates the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CDs in the range of 200–600 nm. The absorption peak in the region of 230–240 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of carbon with sp2 hybridization and absorption in the region of 350 nm is related to the n-π* transition in the edges of the carbon network. Recent studies suggest that absorption in this region primarily originates from the molecular fluorophore transitions, which, for CDs based on citric acid and ethylenediamine, are citrazinic acid or its derivatives (Schneider et al., 2017). The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the synthesized CDs are also shown in Fig. 3a. According to these spectra, the synthesized CDs exhibit blue emission with a wavelength of 450 nm and excitation at 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principal and mechanism of bisulfite detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1, illustrates the structure of TMB and its oxidation stages. As mentioned, MnO2 NSs oxidize TMB without the need for hydrogen peroxide. In this reaction, initially, oxygen in an acidic environment is transformed into the superoxide radical (O2 •-) by accepting electron from MnO2 NSs. This oxygen radical then oxidizes TMB. With an increase in the concentration of MnO2 NSs, the oxidation reaction progresses, resulting in a color change of the solution from colorless (TMB) to blue (diamine/diimine complex), then to green, and finally to yellow (fully oxidized TMB) (Peng et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2018). The UV-Vis spectrum in Fig. 3b shows a decrease in absorbance peak at 652 nm and an increase at 450 nm during the color transformation from blue to yellow.

In this setup, MnO2 NSs oxidizes the TMB compound. The molar ratio of MnO2 NSs to TMB is chosen in order to induce a two-electron oxidation mechanism, and TMB completely turns into the yellow oxidized diimine state. In this state, the oxidized TMB (OxTMB) quenches the fluorescence emission of CDs through the IFE mechanism (Peng et al., 2023). Fig. 3c illustrates the overlap between the absorption spectrum of OxTMB and the emission spectrum of CDs. It is worth noting that MnO2 NSs themselves initially cause the quenching of CDs' fluorescence. However, in the first stage, due to the reaction with TMB, MnO2 NSs are decomposed. In the next stage, in the presence of bisulfite, OxTMB is reduced back to TMB, disrupting the IFE mechanism and consequently recovering the fluorescence emission of CDs. Fig. 3d shows the fluorescence spectrum of CDs when OxTMB and bisulfite are present.

In fluorescence methodologies, increasing the difference in fluorescence signal between the reference (F0) and the sample (F1) enhances sensitivity and detection limits. Therefore, to achieve the maximum sensitivity, parameters influencing the intensity of CDs' fluorescence signal, including the MnO2 NSs to TMB molar ratio, TMB concentration, pH, and reaction time, were optimized.

As the concentration of MnO2 NSs increases, the oxidation reaction of TMB is progressed. Consequently, the color of the solution changes from colorless (unoxidized TMB) into blue (diamine/diimine complex), then to green, and finally to yellow (OxTMB). During the transformation from blue to yellow, the absorbance peak at 652 nm decreases, while the one at 450 nm increases. Therefore, to achieve the maximum quenching of CDs emission, the MnO2 NSs to TMB molar ratio must be adjusted so that TMB is completely transformed into the yellow OxTMB form, resulting in the maximum signal changes with varying MnO2 NSs concentrations. Fig. S2 illustrates the changes in fluorescence intensity of the CDs solution (6.5 mg L−1) and TMB (32 μM) in the presence of various concentrations of MnO2 NSs. According to this graph, a concentration of 44.6 μM of MnO2 NSs, equivalent to an optimal molar ratio of 7–5, was selected.

Increasing the concentration of TMB leads to an increase in the OxTMB concentration, thereby enhancing the IFE mechanism in the quenching of CDs. Fig. S3 shows the variations in the signal of the CDs solution (6.5 mg L−1) in the presence of different concentrations of TMB and a 7:5 molar ratio of MnO2 NSs:TMB. According to this graph, a concentration of 26.7 μM of TMB was chosen as the optimal concentration.

The single-electron and two-electron oxidation reactions of TMB progress with decreasing pH. The optimal pH is the one where the oxidation reaction of TMB to the yellow form exhibits the highest yield, thus showing the maximum quenching of CDs emission. Therefore, it is expected that with an increase in pH, the difference in fluorescence intensity between the reference (F0) and the sample (F1) will decrease, leading to a reduction in sensitivity. Fig. S4 illustrates the difference in fluorescence intensity between F0 and F1 at different pHs for the CDs solution (6.5 mg L−1) in the presence of MnO2 NSs (37.1 μM) and TMB (26.7 μM) (7:5). According to this graph, NaAc-HAc buffer with pH=4.0 was selected as the optimal condition.

To investigate the reaction rate between bisulfite and the OxTMB, F0 and F1 containing 0.5 mg L−1 of bisulfite ion were examined over a time range of 30–300 s. Both solutions contained OxTMB and CDs with a concentration of 26.7 μM and 6.5 mg L−1, respectively. According to Fig. S5, a reaction time of 30 s was selected as the optimal value.

For the investigation of the reaction rate between MnO2 NSs and TMB, the fluorescence signal of the solution containing both TMB (26.7 μM) and MnO2 NSs (37.1 μM), along with CDs (6.5 mg L−1), was recorded over a time range of 1–5 min. As Fig. S6 demonstrates the reaction is complete in the initial minutes; therefore, a reaction time of 1 min was chosen as the optimal time.

In order to assess the selectivity of the method towards various cations and anions and organic compounds as potential interfering species, the setup was examined under optimal conditions. In this study, concentrations of the possible interfering compounds were selected at 10 and 100 times of the bisulfite concentration. As Fig. 4 shows, the oxidizing compounds such as H2O2 and NO2 - do not produce a significant signal because TMB has been completely converted into OxTMB by MnO2 NSs, and no further oxidation is possible. However, reducing compounds such as AA and GSH can produce a rather significant signal when their quantities are ten times that of bisulfite.

The analytical parameters of the method were investigated under optimal conditions at pH=4, MnO2 NSs (37.1 μM), TMB (26.7 μM), and a reaction time of 30 s. The linear dynamic range (LDR) of the method was determined by plotting calibration curves in aqueous media. The calculated analytical data include the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ), linear range, regression coefficient (R2), and relative standard deviation (RSD) listed in Table 1. The LOD and LOQ values were obtained considering a S/N of 3 and 10, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of bisulfite in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of the method in determination of bisulfite in real samples (solid and liquid) was evaluated under optimal condition. The data related to the analysis of liquid samples, including water and white vinegar, are shown in Table 2, while the data for the analysis of solid samples, including sugar, macaroni, and vermicelli, are given in Table 3. According to the results, no bisulfite ion was detected in tap and mineral water, as bisulfite reacts with dissolved oxygen over time and diminishes. In sample A of white vinegar, produced in the year 2023, the detected bisulfite content matches the reported value on the package label. However, in sample B of white vinegar, produced in the year 2021 and approaching its expiration date, the bisulfite content has been significantly decreased, demonstrating the preservation function of bisulfite. Regarding vermicelli and macaroni samples, the presence of more than 10 mg kg−1 of bisulfite is not a concern, as these food items are not consumed raw, and the bisulfite ions are converted to SO2 gas and released due to the cooking process. Additionally, the results of the student t-test indicate that the calculated t-value falls within the 95 % confidence interval of the critical value (tcrit = 4.30), showing no significant difference between the added and found values. Therefore, the relative recovery percentage (RR%) and the t-test demonstrate that the current method has good potential for bisulfite determination in quality control of foodstuff.
Table 4 compares our method with previous relevant fluorescence reports regarding the bisulfite determination. Based on this comparison, the LOD and LOQ values of the current method is acceptable.

In this research, a fluorescence method based on CDs,MnO2 NSs and TMB was developed for determination of bisulfite in variety of food samples. The MnO2 NSs oxidize TMB, quenching the fluorescence emission of CDs. In the presence of bisulfite, OxTMB is reduced, leading to the recovery of CDs emission. Thus, bisulfite could be identified via the fluorescence signal of CDs. Reaction of bisulfite ions with the OxTMB instead of MnO2 NSs, enhanced the method’s response time and sensitivity. The method was successfully utilized for bisulfite measurement in liquid food samples such as water and vinegar, as well as solid samples including sugar and macaroni. The detection limit of method was 0.06 mg L−1 and the permissible limit of bisulfite in food items is set at 10 mg kg−1 according to the standards of Australia, Canada, the United States, and Europe. Therefore, the developed setup, considering its very fast response time, desirable sensitivity, and selectivity, holds great potential for quality control in the food industry. The performance metrics of the developed method also demonstrate its effectiveness compared to similar previous methods.

Farbod Bayat-Afshary: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Habib Bagheri: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Delaram-Sadat Tavoussi-Shirazi: Validation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Habib Bagheri reports financial support was provided by Sharif University of Technology. Habib Bagheri reports a relationship with Sharif University of Technology that includes: employment. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors greatly appreciate the support from Sharif University of Technology (Grant no. QA990417).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.106538.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ali et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One pot fabrication of fluorescein functionalized manganese dioxide for fluorescence “Turn OFF–ON” sensing of hydrogen peroxide in water and cosmetic samples
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ali, H.R.H., Hassan, A.I., Hassan, Y.F., El-Wekil, M.M., (2020). One pot fabrication of fluorescein functionalized manganese dioxide for fluorescence “Turn OFF–ON” sensing of hydrogen peroxide in water and cosmetic samples. RSC advances 10(30), 17506-17514.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Amjadi et al., 2018
